Reed: I have your white cornered and surrounded
Reed: I thought that constituted the capture
Reed: The ones to the right of mine.
newbold: you can refer to a specific stone by its position on the board, e.g. R14
newbold: are you talking about S16, T16, S15 ?
Reed: Yes, and the others on 18
newbold: which would be the condition required to capture
newbold: they have 'liberties' -- open nearby spaces
newbold: R19, T19, S17, T17, etc.
Reed: I have to capture all of the liberties?
newbold: those are all spaces that would need to be occupied in order to capture
newbold: yes, that's the actual definition of a capture --- ""when all liberties are removed""
newbold: works for both single stones and entire groups of stones
Reed: Would I capture them if you placed stones in each liberty?
newbold: sort of... it could work that way, or at least almost that way :)
newbold: I could never place *all* of the stones required for you to capture
newbold: as the last stone I'd place would constitute 'suicide' on my part
newbold: which is an illegal move
newbold: (the game wouldn't let me do it)
newbold: ko is something a little different
Reed: So, I would place it there and it would capture.
newbold: in this case, look at R16 and R15
Reed: Which you placed a stone at
newbold: so, here, don't accept my undo request
newbold: instead, try putting a stone at Q15 yourself
C[newbold: congrats, you've added two liberties :)
newbold: when you place a stone, think about how many liberties you're adding
newbold: avoid placing a stone that doesn't change the overall liberty count... it's usually a wasted stone
newbold: now this here is an interesting situation
newbold: someone's going to lose some stones, but it depends on how it plays out :)
C[newbold: First tip, take a moment to read things, count liberties, etc
Reed: Do you mind kind of explaining to me what it takes to capture?
Reed: I know HOW to capture.
Reed: But a few moves I made I thought were going to capture
newbold: gotta get my keyboard (using a tablet now)
newbold: when did you think you were going to capture?
newbold: if you want, we can undo and go back to that point
newbold: I could have played P15 or Q14...
newbold: but I'll try something else instead and avoid the conflict :)
newbold: now, you have a nice opportunity to threaten me here
Reed: Heh, not even in the slightest.
newbold: ok, take a look at P16, Q16
Reed: I was going to take out your liberties within that area
newbold: how many liberties do I have there?
newbold: so, if you place a stone in one of them, you'll threaten to capture me
Reed: So, O16 is my best move
newbold: we'll play it out and then back up
Reed: But, won't that open up more liberties since it will create a much bigger unit?
newbold: connected stones are stronger than separated stones
newbold: either O16 or P15 would threaten me
newbold: but P15 is the one that connects to your existing stones :)
newbold: also, notice how if *I* had played O15, it'd threaten your three stones?
newbold: there's a very helpful Go proverb: ""your opponent's best move is also your best move""
Reed: Since I've only two liberties.
newbold: so, this situation here, where my P16 and Q16 stones have 1 remaining liberty, is called ""atari""
newbold: you want to try to put people in atari... it forces them to respond, and keeps the game flowing to your advantage
newbold: if I don't play O16, I'll lose those stones
C[newbold: because you forced me to make a move, you're now free to choose where to play

newbold: and then things get really messy :)
newbold: a very helpful skill to develop early on is identifying dead stones and cutting your  losses
newbold: another Go saying: ""don't throw good stones after bad""
newbold: everything connected to P14 is dead now

newbold: at this point, you generally have two goals:
newbold: 1. invade your opponent's territory to increase your own
newbold: both of those attempts kind of failed :(

C[newbold: 'dead' means ""can't be made to live""
newbold: so when you see stones that you know are going to get crushed, they're 'dead'

newbold: so they can be considered dead at this point
newbold: but, in contrast, your huge group in the lower left

newbold: same with your other shapes
